question_text: Let X1, X2 be a random sample from a distribution having probability density function fθ(x) = θe−x/θ, x > 0, otherwise, where θ ∈(0, ∞) is an unknown parameter. For testing H0 : θ ≤1 against H1 : θ > 1,

options.C: onsider the test ϕ(X1, X2) = if X1 > 1, otherwise. Then which one of the following tests has the same power function as ϕ?
options.A: ϕ1(X1, X2) = X1+X2−1 X1+X2 , if X1 + X2 > 1, otherwise.
options.B: ϕ2(X1, X2) = 2X1+2X2−1 2(X1+X2) , if X1 + X2 > 1, otherwise.
options.D: ϕ4(X1, X2) = 4X1+4X2−1 4(X1+X2) , if X1 + X2 > 1, otherwise.

solution:
Step 1: Understand the original test ϕ(X1, X2).
The test ϕ(X1, X2) is defined as:
ϕ(X1, X2) =
if X1 > 1,
otherwise.
This test is checking whether X1 is greater than 1, and it essentially ignores the value of X2.
Step 2: Understand the power function of ϕ(X1, X2).
The power function of a test gives the probability of rejecting the null hypothesis H0 (i.e., the
test statistic is greater than a critical value) for various values of the parameter θ.
For θ = 1 (the null hypothesis), the probability of rejecting H0 is:
P(X1 > 1 | θ = 1) =
Z ∞
1e−x/1 dx = e−1.
For θ > 1, the probability of rejecting H0 is:
P(X1 > 1 | θ) = 1 −FX(1) = 1 −(1 −e−1/θ) = e−1/θ.
Step 3: Analyze the options.
Option (A): This test depends on X1 + X2, and it has the same form as the original test
because the sum of X1 and X2 will determine the power function in a way that matches the
original test for large θ. Hence, option (A) is correct.
Option (B), (C), and (D): These tests differ in the factors used in the formula, which would
result in different power functions that do not match the power function of the original test ϕ.
Thus, the correct answer is (A) .
Quick Tip
When analyzing tests with different forms, focus on how the power function is derived
and check if the form of the test aligns with the original test’s power function.
